title: Magnetars
subtitle: Neutron stars with a magnetic field a trillion times stronger than Earth's. The most magnetized objects in the universe — and the most dangerous.

hero.prompt: Photorealistic scientific illustration of a magnetar: a compact neutron star with an intensely luminous surface radiating blue-white X-ray glow, powerful twisted magnetic field lines shown as glowing amber-red curves spiraling outward from magnetic poles, a brilliant burst of gamma rays erupting from the surface in a narrow cone. Deep black space background with faint distant star field. Hard sci-fi style, dark space background. Add the following text labels on the image: "magnetar", "magnetic field lines", "gamma-ray burst", "neutron star crust".
hero.alt: A magnetar — a compact neutron star with twisted ultra-strong magnetic field lines and a gamma-ray flare erupting from the surface
hero.caption: A magnetar is a neutron star with a magnetic field reaching 100 trillion gauss. In December 2004, one such object 50,000 light-years away disrupted Earth's ionosphere.

paragraphs: In December 2004, radiation detectors aboard space telescopes throughout the Solar System simultaneously failed — they were simply not designed for the incoming flux. In one tenth of a second, a small object in the constellation Sagittarius, located 50,000 light-years from us, released more energy than the Sun emits over 250,000 years. The wave of gamma radiation reached Earth and visibly ionized the upper atmosphere — from a distance nearly twice the diameter of the Galaxy from edge to edge. The culprit was **magnetar SGR 1806-20**.

Magnetars are a subclass of neutron stars with magnetic fields ranging from 100 billion to 100 trillion gauss. For comparison: Earth's magnetic field is approximately 0.5 gauss, and a typical refrigerator magnet produces tens of gauss. Even an ordinary neutron star carries a field of around one trillion gauss — and still falls short of a magnetar by hundreds of billions of times. Only about thirty such objects are known in the entire Galaxy. Yet in terms of their reach and impact, they have no rivals among stars.

image.prompt: Scientific infographic comparing magnetic field strengths on a logarithmic scale: horizontal bar chart with labeled values from left (weakest) to right (strongest). Entries: Earth magnetic field 0.5 gauss, refrigerator magnet 100 gauss, MRI machine 50000 gauss, normal neutron star 10^12 gauss, magnetar 10^14 to 10^15 gauss. Each bar colored differently: blue for Earth, green for magnet, yellow for MRI, cyan for neutron star, red-orange for magnetar. Hard sci-fi style, dark background, monospace labels. Add the following text labels on the image: "Earth 0.5 G", "neutron star 10^12 G", "magnetar 10^15 G", "log scale".
image.alt: Logarithmic scale of magnetic field strengths from Earth's field to a magnetar's
image.caption: A magnetar's magnetic field is ten quadrillion times stronger than Earth's. Even from several hundred kilometers away, it would distort atoms — pulling electron clouds into narrow cylinders aligned along the field lines.

body.heading: The Neutron Star: A Starting Point
paragraphs: To understand a magnetar, you first need to understand a neutron star. When a massive star (from roughly eight to about twenty-five solar masses) explodes as a supernova, its core collapses in a fraction of a second. The pressure is so extreme that protons and electrons merge into neutrons. The result is a sphere roughly 20 kilometers across with a mass of one to two solar masses. This is the neutron star — an object denser than an atomic nucleus.

A young neutron star spins rapidly — up to hundreds of rotations per second — and carries a powerful magnetic field inherited from its parent star and compressed along with it. When we observe such a star as a regular pulsed radio signal, we call it a pulsar. The best-known example is the pulsar inside the Crab Nebula, the remnant of a supernova that Chinese and Arab astronomers recorded in the eleventh century: it spins 33 times per second and radiates across the full spectrum, from radio waves to gamma rays.

A magnetar differs from a pulsar in one key respect: its magnetic field is a thousand times stronger or more. Where this difference comes from is a question physicists took several decades to resolve.

body.heading: How the Ultra-Strong Field is Born
paragraphs: The answer involves the dynamo mechanism — the same process that sustains Earth's magnetic field. In Earth's core, convection of liquid metal combined with planetary rotation generates and maintains the field. Something similar, but in incomparably more extreme conditions, occurs in the first seconds after a neutron star is born.

Immediately after collapse, the young neutron star is an extraordinarily hot, turbulent, convective ball of proto-neutron matter. If it spins fast enough — with an initial period shorter than one second — the convective flows of neutron fluid, combined with rotation, can power a strong dynamo. Computer simulations show that this dynamo can build the magnetic field up to 10 to the power of 15 gauss — the characteristic magnetar value — within the first few seconds of the star's life.

Why don't all neutron stars become magnetars? Most likely it comes down to the initial rotation speed of the progenitor star and the conditions of the collapse. If the proto-neutron star spins too slowly, the dynamo never reaches full power — and an ordinary pulsar forms rather than a magnetar.

diagram.caption: Magnetar magnetic field lines form a complex dipolar structure. Unlike ordinary pulsars, a magnetar's magnetic axis can be strongly tilted relative to the rotation axis, and the field actively evolves — cracking the crust as it does so.

body.heading: Two Faces of Magnetars
paragraphs: Astronomers discovered magnetars through two distinct observational manifestations, and only later realized that the same type of object lay behind both.

The first type is **soft gamma repeaters**. These are sources that periodically emit short bursts of soft gamma radiation and X-rays. Unlike classical gamma-ray bursts — which are one-time events — soft gamma repeaters fire again and again; dozens or even thousands of bursts can occur within hours or days. This is the class to which SGR 1806-20 belongs, the object that staged its planetary spectacle in December 2004.

The second type is **anomalous X-ray pulsars**. These emit X-ray radiation far more luminous than can be accounted for by the loss of rotational energy alone. That surplus energy comes from the magnetic field — hence "anomalous." They rotate slowly (periods of several to ten seconds per rotation), another telltale sign: the powerful field efficiently brakes the star's spin.

Both classes are now unified under the single label "magnetars." The boundary between them blurred when some anomalous X-ray pulsars started producing bursts, and some soft gamma repeaters fell into extended X-ray silence.

image.prompt: Scientific illustration of a magnetar giant flare event: a compact neutron star at center radiating an enormous expanding fireball of plasma and gamma radiation, bright shockwave rings expanding outward in concentric circles, intense white-blue X-ray jets perpendicular to the magnetic axis. Hard sci-fi style, dark space background, dramatic lighting. Add the following text labels on the image: "magnetar SGR 1806-20", "giant flare December 2004", "gamma radiation", "expanding fireball".
image.alt: Giant flare from magnetar SGR 1806-20 — an expanding fireball of gamma radiation
image.caption: The December 2004 giant flare from SGR 1806-20 was the most energetic such event in observational history. Gamma-ray telescope detectors saturated in under a second. The wave of ionizing radiation measurably affected Earth's ionosphere at night, comparable to daytime solar illumination.

body.heading: Starquakes and the Mechanism Behind the Flares
paragraphs: A magnetar is not a static object. Its ultra-strong magnetic field continuously evolves, and the magnetic stress stored in it seeks release. The analogy is tectonic — but instead of rock, the material is the crystalline crust of a neutron star, roughly one kilometer thick and a trillion times denser than lead.

As the magnetic field shifts and reorganizes inside the star, it drags the crust with it. The crust deforms elastically — and then suddenly fractures. This is a **starquake**: a crack propagating through the crystal lattice of neutron-rich material at the speed of sound in ultradense matter. If we could assign these quakes a Richter magnitude, the 2004 giant flare would register around 32. The strongest earthquake in recorded human history was magnitude 9.5.

The sudden displacement of the crust disturbs the magnetic field above the surface and triggers an avalanche process in the magnetosphere. Field lines break and reconnect, releasing stored magnetic energy as an explosive burst of gamma photons and charged particles. For the largest flares, this energy is released in a fraction of a second — followed by a gradually decaying tail lasting minutes.

body.heading: December 2004: The Earthquake That Reached Earth
paragraphs: At 21:30 UTC on 27 December 2004, the giant flare from SGR 1806-20 reached Earth. The spacecraft RHESSI recorded a gamma-ray flux one hundred times greater than the previous record from any extrasolar source. The telescopes INTEGRAL and Ulysses also detected the event — their detectors saturated almost instantaneously.

But the most remarkable effect was on Earth itself. The wave of ionizing radiation energized the nightside ionosphere to levels normally seen only in daylight. This was confirmed by very-low-frequency radio monitoring stations around the world — frequencies used to communicate with submarines suddenly reflected off the atmosphere in an altered pattern. Had such a magnetar been located within a few thousand light-years of us — rather than 50,000 — the consequences for the biosphere would have been catastrophic.

image.prompt: Scientific diagram showing the effect of a magnetar giant flare on Earth's ionosphere: Earth shown from space with a layered atmosphere, the ionosphere layer highlighted in blue glowing outline, a gamma-ray pulse shown as orange-red wave arriving from distant space and interacting with the ionosphere layer causing it to glow brighter. On the night side of Earth, the ionosphere shows elevated ionization matching daytime levels. Hard sci-fi style, dark background. Add the following text labels on the image: "magnetar SGR 1806-20", "50000 light-years", "gamma-ray pulse", "ionosphere effect".
image.alt: Effect of the gamma-ray wave from magnetar SGR 1806-20 on Earth's ionosphere
image.caption: The gamma-ray wavefront from SGR 1806-20 ionized Earth's nightside ionosphere, confirmed by very-low-frequency radio monitoring stations. This remains the most energetic extrasolar event recorded in observational astrophysics.

body.heading: Fast Radio Bursts: Magnetars Ring the Bell
paragraphs: Through the first decade of the twenty-first century, radio astronomers began finding puzzling ultra-bright pulses of radio emission lasting from a millisecond to a few seconds, arriving mostly from extragalactic distances. They were named **fast radio bursts**. For years their origin was completely opaque: in the time one lasts, the source manages to radiate in radio as much energy as the Sun emits over tens of thousands of years.

The key breakthrough came in April 2020. The Canadian telescope CHIME and several other observatories detected a powerful fast radio burst from within our own Galaxy. The source was precisely localized — it turned out to be the known magnetar SGR 1935+2154. This burst, now designated FRB 200428, matched extragalactic fast radio bursts in structure and characteristics — only weaker, because the source was nearby (roughly 30,000 light-years away). Had this burst originated in a neighboring galaxy, it would have appeared as a typical extragalactic fast radio burst.

This discovery did not prove that all fast radio bursts come from magnetars, but it provided powerful evidence that at least some of them do. Today the magnetar model is considered the leading explanation for the majority of these events.

diagram.caption: Between an ordinary pulsar and a magnetar there are three orders of magnitude in field strength. Between Earth and a magnetar, fifteen orders. This cannot be visualized on a linear scale — hence logarithmic.

body.heading: Why So Few, and Why They Are So Hard to Find
paragraphs: Magnetars live briefly. An ultra-strong magnetic field is an enormous energy reservoir, and the star burns through it rapidly. Calculations indicate that the active magnetar phase lasts only a few thousand to roughly ten thousand years. After that the field weakens, the bursts stop, and the object becomes an ordinary but slowly rotating pulsar — or simply fades from view. Over the full history of the Milky Way, many magnetars could have formed, but the overwhelming majority have long gone quiet.

Active magnetars reveal themselves through X-ray and gamma-ray bursts, but between outbursts they are faint and easily missed. Some have been found by orbital X-ray telescopes scanning the galactic plane. Others were discovered by accident — a burst happened to saturate a detector that was pointing at an entirely different target.

Another search strategy is to look for supernova remnants that lack a visible pulsar at their centers. If the nebula is there but the classical radio pulsar is not, it is worth checking whether a quiet magnetar is hiding inside. This approach has turned up several candidates. In total, as of the mid-2020s, approximately thirty magnetars have been confirmed or proposed in our Galaxy — and there are probably just as many still undetected.

image.prompt: Scientific illustration of a magnetar detected by X-ray space telescope: a bright point source of X-ray emission shown in false-color blue-violet against the dark galactic plane, surrounded by a faint supernova remnant shell glowing in red-orange, other dim stars in the galactic background. Hard sci-fi style, false-color X-ray style visualization, dark background. Add the following text labels on the image: "magnetar X-ray source", "supernova remnant", "galactic plane", "X-ray telescope view".
image.alt: X-ray view of a magnetar — a bright point source inside a supernova remnant against the galactic plane
image.caption: Most magnetars have been found by orbital X-ray telescopes: XMM-Newton, Chandra, and Swift. Between active outbursts they emit tens to hundreds of times less — and frequently go unnoticed.

body.heading: Open Questions and Future Prospects
paragraphs: Despite decades of study, magnetars remain objects with many unresolved questions. One of the most pressing concerns the precise structure of the neutron star interior. Is there a layer of superfluid neutron liquid beneath the crystalline crust? What is the equation of state of ultradense matter — that is, how do pressure and temperature relate to density in these conditions? The answer determines how efficiently the dynamo operates and what maximum field strength a magnetar can reach.

Another open question concerns the relationship between magnetars and fast radio bursts. The detection of FRB 200428 in April 2020 confirmed the magnetar origin for at least one Galactic burst. But do all extragalactic fast radio bursts share the same origin? Some repeat; others do not. There may be several distinct generation mechanisms, with magnetars as just one of them.

Finally, there is the question of the role magnetars play in the synthesis of heavy elements. Neutron star mergers are accompanied by a kilonova burst, during which gold, platinum, and other heavy elements are forged. Some models suggest that a magnetar born in such a merger can substantially boost the energy and the yield of heavy nuclei. Confirmation or refutation of these models is expected from future gravitational-wave observations paired with X-ray and optical telescopes.

image.prompt: Scientific illustration of a fast radio burst originating from a magnetar: a compact neutron star (magnetar) at center with intense magnetic field lines, a brilliant millisecond-duration coherent radio pulse shown as an expanding bright ring of blue-white electromagnetic radiation propagating outward, the pulse crossing interstellar medium represented by faint wisps of ionized gas. Hard sci-fi style, dark space background, dramatic lighting. Add the following text labels on the image: "magnetar SGR 1935+2154", "fast radio burst FRB 200428", "radio pulse", "April 2020".
image.alt: Fast radio burst from magnetar SGR 1935+2154 — an expanding ring-shaped radio pulse
image.caption: April 2020: the CHIME and STARE2 telescopes recorded a powerful fast radio burst from the Galactic magnetar SGR 1935+2154. This is the first direct evidence of the magnetar origin of fast radio bursts.

glossary.definition: A subclass of neutron star with a magnetic field from 10 to the power of 14 to 10 to the power of 15 gauss — the most magnetized known objects in the universe. They remain active for only a few thousand years.
The ultradense remnant left after a massive star explodes as a supernova, composed mainly of neutrons. Roughly 20 kilometers in diameter, mass one to two solar masses. Denser than an atomic nucleus.
An astronomical source that produces repeated bursts of soft gamma radiation and X-rays. Now understood to be one observational class of magnetars.
A pulsar whose X-ray luminosity greatly exceeds what can be supplied by the loss of rotational energy. The surplus comes from the magnetic field. The second observational class of magnetars.
A sudden fracture in the crystalline crust of a neutron star driven by magnetic stress. It triggers a reorganization of the magnetic field and an eruption of gamma radiation.

An extraordinarily bright radio pulse lasting from a millisecond to a few seconds, arriving mostly from extragalactic distances. The energy in a single burst rivals the Sun's entire radio output over tens of thousands of years.

The mechanism that generates and sustains a magnetic field through the motion of electrically conducting fluid. In magnetars it is driven by convection of proto-neutron matter during the first seconds after the star's birth.
The most energetic variety of magnetar outburst, releasing thousands of times more energy than a typical burst. Only a handful have ever been recorded — in 1979, 1998, and 2004.
The outermost crystalline layer of a neutron star, roughly one kilometer thick. Composed mainly of neutron-rich nuclear material. Its fracture is the trigger for starquakes.

quiz.question: What magnetic field strength is typical of a magnetar?
options: One to one hundred gauss — similar to a permanent magnet
Around one trillion gauss — the same as an ordinary neutron star
From one hundred billion to one hundred trillion gauss
More than one quadrillion gauss — beyond any known neutron star
quiz.explanation: Magnetars carry fields of 10 to the power of 14 to 10 to the power of 15 gauss — three orders of magnitude stronger than ordinary neutron stars at around 10 to the power of 12 gauss. This is the strongest magnetic field known in the universe.

quiz.question: What triggers a gamma-ray flare in a magnetar?
options: Accretion of matter from a companion star, as in X-ray binary systems
Merger of two neutron stars in a binary system
A starquake — a sudden fracture of the crystalline crust driven by magnetic stress
A thermonuclear flash on the surface, as in X-ray bursters
quiz.explanation: The magnetar's magnetic field shifts and reorganizes, dragging the crystalline crust with it. When the crust suddenly breaks — a starquake — the shock disturbs field lines above the surface, causing them to reconnect and release gamma photons.

quiz.question: What was the significance of the detection of FRB 200428 in April 2020?
options: It was the first fast radio burst detected outside the Milky Way
It was the first direct confirmation that at least some fast radio bursts originate from magnetars
It was the first repeating fast radio burst from a known source
It was the first fast radio burst detected simultaneously by two independent telescopes
quiz.explanation: FRB 200428 was localized to the Galactic magnetar SGR 1935+2154. This provided the first direct evidence that magnetars can produce fast radio bursts — though whether all extragalactic bursts share this origin remains an open question.

quiz.question: Why are only about thirty magnetars known?
options: Magnetars form only through neutron star mergers, which are rare events
The active magnetar phase lasts only a few thousand years, after which the field weakens and the object fades from view
Magnetars form only in dwarf galaxies, not in the Milky Way
Magnetars are too hot to be observed in the X-ray band
quiz.explanation: The ultra-strong magnetic field is a large energy reservoir that depletes rapidly. The active phase lasts a few thousand to roughly ten thousand years. After that the star goes quiet, and finding it becomes very difficult. Far more magnetars could have formed throughout Galactic history than are currently known.

quiz.question: What primarily distinguishes a magnetar from an ordinary pulsar?
options: A magnetar rotates far faster — more than a thousand times per second
A magnetar sits at the center of an active galaxy, not in a supernova remnant
A magnetar has a magnetic field a thousand or more times stronger, and its energy comes primarily from that field rather than from spin-down
A magnetar is composed of quark-gluon plasma rather than neutrons
quiz.explanation: The defining difference is field strength and the source of energy. An ordinary pulsar radiates by losing rotational energy through spin-down. A magnetar draws its extraordinary activity from its stored magnetic energy — a much larger reservoir that is released unpredictably.
